meta.note: Plain-English function and key rule for every section. linked_module points to the gated module screen ID where that section is treated in depth.

sections.heading: Short title, extent and commencement
sections.function: Names the Act, declares it applies throughout Pakistan, and states when it came into force.
sections.key_rule: The CrPC applies to all criminal proceedings in Pakistan unless a specific law provides otherwise.

sections.heading: Definitions
sections.function: Defines the core vocabulary that governs the entire Code — cognizable, non-cognizable, bailable, warrant case, summons case, and more.
sections.key_rule: These definitions are load-bearing: cognizable/non-cognizable determines the investigation track; bailable/non-bailable determines the bail track; warrant/summons case determines the trial track.
sections.note: S.2 is the gateway to everything else. Misreading a definition here produces a wrong answer on every section that flows from it.

sections.heading: Trial of offences under Pakistan Penal Code
sections.function: Provides that offences under the PPC shall be investigated, inquired into, tried and otherwise dealt with according to the CrPC.
sections.key_rule: PPC offences + CrPC procedure = the standard pairing. A specific law can displace CrPC, but only by explicit provision.

sections.heading: Trial of offences against other laws
sections.function: Extends the CrPC procedure to offences under any other law, subject to that law's own specific procedural provisions.
sections.key_rule: CrPC is the default procedural code for all criminal offences unless the creating statute provides a different procedure.

sections.heading: Saving
sections.function: Preserves any special form of procedure applicable under any existing law at the time the CrPC was enacted.
sections.key_rule: Special procedures in older legislation survive the CrPC unless expressly repealed.

chapters.title: Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices

sections.heading: Classes of criminal courts
sections.function: Establishes the hierarchy of criminal courts: High Court, Sessions Court, Judicial Magistrates (First, Second, Third Class), and Executive Magistrates.
sections.key_rule: The court's class determines its sentencing jurisdiction. Getting the hierarchy wrong affects every question about which court tries which offence.
sections.note: The distinction between Judicial Magistrates and Executive Magistrates is critical — Executive Magistrates have no trial jurisdiction and can only exercise specific administrative powers.

sections.heading: Sessions Divisions and Sessions Judges
sections.function: Divides Pakistan into Sessions Divisions; each has a Sessions Judge appointed by the High Court.
sections.key_rule: Sessions Judge has original jurisdiction over capital offences and appellate jurisdiction over Magistrate convictions.

sections.heading: Additional and Assistant Sessions Judges
sections.function: Provides for appointment of Additional Sessions Judges with the same powers as the Sessions Judge, and Assistant Sessions Judges with limited powers.
sections.key_rule: Additional Sessions Judge has full Sessions Court powers. Assistant Sessions Judge cannot try offences punishable with death.

sections.heading: Court of Session — how held
sections.function: Specifies that the Sessions Court shall be held at such places as the High Court may appoint.
sections.key_rule: Sessions Court sits at fixed places designated by the High Court — it is not ambulatory by default.

sections.heading: Submission of returns to High Court
sections.function: Requires Sessions Judges to submit statistical returns to the High Court as directed.
sections.key_rule: Administrative reporting obligation — not a procedural provision with substantive legal effect.

sections.heading: Chief Judicial Magistrate
sections.function: Designates the Chief Judicial Magistrate as the head of the Magistracy in a district, with supervisory powers over other Magistrates.
sections.key_rule: Chief Judicial Magistrate can withdraw cases from subordinate Magistrates and recall cases for trial himself.

sections.heading: Judicial Magistrates
sections.function: Establishes three classes of Judicial Magistrate with progressively limited sentencing jurisdiction.
sections.key_rule: First Class: up to 3 years + fine. Second Class: up to 1 year + fine. Third Class: up to 1 month + fine.

sections.heading: Special Judicial Magistrates
sections.function: Allows the Provincial Government to appoint persons as Special Judicial Magistrates for specific classes of cases.
sections.key_rule: Special Judicial Magistrates have only the powers specifically conferred by their appointment — not all Magistrate powers automatically.

sections.heading: Local jurisdiction of Judicial Magistrates
sections.function: Defines the territorial limits within which a Judicial Magistrate may exercise jurisdiction.
sections.key_rule: A Magistrate has jurisdiction over offences committed within his district or sub-division. Acting outside territorial limits vitiates proceedings.

sections.heading: Subordination of Judicial Magistrates
sections.function: Establishes that all Judicial Magistrates in a district are subordinate to the Sessions Judge and the Chief Judicial Magistrate.
sections.key_rule: The Sessions Judge may withdraw any case from a subordinate Magistrate and either try it himself or transfer it.

sections.heading: Executive Magistrates
sections.function: Creates a separate cadre of Executive Magistrates who exercise administrative and preventive powers but have no trial jurisdiction.
sections.key_rule: Executive Magistrates try no cases. Their CrPC powers are preventive — security proceedings, unlawful assembly, public nuisance, S.144 orders.
sections.note: Confusing Executive and Judicial Magistrates is a persistent error. Executive Magistrates cannot try criminal cases.

sections.heading: District Magistrate
sections.function: Designates the District Magistrate as the head of the Executive Magistracy in a district.
sections.key_rule: District Magistrate has the broadest Executive Magistrate powers including S.144 orders and security proceedings.

sections.heading: Sub-Divisional Magistrates
sections.function: Provides for Sub-Divisional Magistrates with Executive Magistrate powers within their sub-division.
sections.key_rule: Sub-Divisional Magistrate's powers are geographically limited to the sub-division.

sections.heading: Special Executive Magistrates
sections.function: Allows Provincial Government to appoint Special Executive Magistrates for specific localities or functions.
sections.key_rule: Powers are limited to what is specifically conferred — not the full range of Executive Magistrate powers.

sections.heading: Subordination of Executive Magistrates
sections.function: Establishes the chain of command within the Executive Magistracy.
sections.key_rule: All Executive Magistrates are subordinate to the District Magistrate, who is subordinate to the Provincial Government.

sections.heading: Magistrates ex-officio
sections.function: Designates certain officers as Magistrates by virtue of their office without separate appointment.
sections.key_rule: Ex-officio Magistrates have the powers of the class their designation carries — not automatically First Class.

sections.heading: Public prosecutors
sections.function: Provides for the appointment of Public Prosecutors by the Provincial Government to conduct criminal prosecutions.
sections.key_rule: Public Prosecutors represent the State. They have an independent duty to act fairly — they are Ministers of Justice, not advocates for conviction at any cost.

sections.heading: Courts by which offences are triable
sections.function: Sets out which courts may try which offences, by reference to the First Schedule.
sections.key_rule: The First Schedule is the master reference for trial jurisdiction. If the schedule says Sessions, only Sessions Court can try it.

sections.heading: Jurisdiction in the case of juveniles
sections.function: Provides that persons under 16 accused of an offence (other than one punishable with death) should ordinarily be tried by a Juvenile Court.
sections.key_rule: Age at the time of the offence, not at the time of trial, determines juvenile jurisdiction.

sections.heading: Sentences which Sessions Judges may pass
sections.function: Authorises Sessions Judges to pass any sentence authorised by law, including death — but death sentences require High Court confirmation.
sections.key_rule: Sessions Court can impose death but cannot execute it without High Court confirmation under S.374.

sections.heading: Sentences which Magistrates may pass
sections.function: Specifies the maximum sentences each class of Magistrate may impose.
sections.key_rule: First Class: 3 years + fine. Second Class: 1 year + fine. Third Class: 1 month + fine. A Magistrate cannot sentence beyond his class limit even if the offence carries more.

sections.heading: Sentences in cases of conviction of several offences at one trial
sections.function: Governs how sentences run when an accused is convicted of multiple offences at the same trial — consecutive or concurrent.
sections.key_rule: Court may direct sentences to run concurrently or consecutively. Total consecutive sentences by a Magistrate cannot exceed 14 years.

sections.heading: Powers of Judges and Magistrates exercisable by their successors
sections.function: Provides that powers exercised by a Judge or Magistrate may be exercised by his successor in office.
sections.key_rule: Judicial continuity — a case does not die with the transfer of the judge. The successor may continue from where the predecessor stopped.

sections.heading: Public when to assist Magistrates and police
sections.function: Places a legal obligation on every person to assist a Magistrate or police officer demanding assistance in preventing breach of peace or arresting an accused.
sections.key_rule: Refusal to assist without reasonable cause is an offence under S.187 PPC.

sections.heading: Public to give information of certain offences
sections.function: Imposes a duty on every person aware of the commission of certain serious offences to report them to the nearest Magistrate or police officer.
sections.key_rule: Failure to report is an offence under S.176 PPC. The duty applies to scheduled offences including offences against the state and offences involving death or serious injury.

sections.heading: Power to pursue into other jurisdictions
sections.function: Allows a police officer pursuing a person in his territorial jurisdiction to continue the pursuit into any other part of Pakistan.
sections.key_rule: Hot pursuit crosses territorial boundaries lawfully. Once the person is apprehended, ordinary production rules apply.

sections.heading: Arrest by Magistrate
sections.function: Empowers a Magistrate to arrest or direct the arrest of any person who commits an offence in his presence, or any person within the Magistrate's local jurisdiction who is wanted under a warrant.
sections.key_rule: A Magistrate can arrest personally — he is not limited to issuing process and waiting. Most commonly invoked when contempt occurs in court.

sections.heading: How arrest is made
sections.function: Specifies the manner of making an arrest — the officer shall actually touch or confine the body of the person to be arrested, unless there is a submission to custody.
sections.key_rule: Words alone are not an arrest. Physical touch or actual submission to custody constitutes the arrest.

sections.heading: Search of place entered by person sought
sections.function: Permits a police officer to search any premises where a person to be arrested has entered or is reasonably believed to have entered.
sections.key_rule: The officer may break open any door or window if refused entry after demanding it. This is a derived search power incident to arrest — not a standalone warrant.

sections.heading: No unnecessary restraint
sections.function: Prohibits subjecting an arrested person to more restraint than is necessary to prevent escape.
sections.key_rule: Proportionality applies to the conduct of arrest independent of whether the arrest itself is lawful. Excessive restraint is independently actionable.
sections.note: S.49 has been invoked in police brutality cases — it operates as a constitutional-adjacent protection on the manner of arrest.

sections.heading: Search of arrested person
sections.function: Permits a police officer to search an arrested person and place in safe custody all articles found, except necessary wearing apparel.
sections.key_rule: Search of arrested person is automatic on arrest — no separate authority is needed. All articles must be itemised and receipted.

sections.heading: Mode of searching women
sections.function: Requires that women be searched only by female officers, with strict decency.
sections.key_rule: A male officer cannot search a female arrested person. Violation is actionable and affects the admissibility of anything found.

sections.heading: Examination of arrested person by medical practitioner
sections.function: Provides for medical examination of an accused at the request of a police officer where examination is likely to afford evidence of an offence.
sections.key_rule: Only a registered medical practitioner may conduct the examination. Most commonly used in rape and assault cases.

sections.heading: When police may arrest without warrant
sections.function: Lists the specific grounds on which a police officer may arrest a person without a warrant — the most important being reasonable suspicion of involvement in a cognizable offence.
sections.key_rule: Nine grounds listed. Any arrest without warrant must be referable to one of these nine grounds. A bare assertion of suspicion is insufficient.
sections.note: S.54 is the statutory foundation for the cognizable/warrantless arrest track.

sections.heading: Refusal to give name and residence
sections.function: Permits a police officer to arrest a person who refuses to give his name and residence when required to do so after committing a non-cognizable offence in the officer's presence.
sections.key_rule: Non-cognizable offence + refusal to identify = arrest power. But the person must be released as soon as name and residence are ascertained.

sections.heading: Arrest by private persons and procedure on such arrest
sections.function: Permits a private person to arrest someone who in his presence commits a non-bailable and cognizable offence, or who is a proclaimed offender.
sections.key_rule: After a private arrest, the person must be handed over to a police officer or taken before a Magistrate without unnecessary delay. A private person cannot detain indefinitely.

sections.heading: Person arrested to be taken before Magistrate or officer in charge of police station
sections.function: Requires that a person arrested without a warrant be brought before a Magistrate having jurisdiction or before the officer in charge of a police station without unnecessary delay.
sections.key_rule: This implements the constitutional 24-hour production requirement under Art. 10(2). No unnecessary delay means as fast as practicable.

sections.heading: Discharge of person apprehended
sections.function: Requires that a person arrested without a warrant be discharged if no order for detention is received from a Magistrate within 24 hours.
sections.key_rule: If a Magistrate does not order detention within 24 hours of production, the person must be released. This is the enforcement mechanism for Art. 10(2).

sections.heading: Power, on escape, to pursue and retake
sections.function: Empowers any person to pursue and retake an arrested person who escapes from custody.
sections.key_rule: Pursuit and retaking is lawful for anyone — not just police officers. The escaped person may be retaken anywhere in Pakistan.

sections.heading: Form of summons
sections.function: Specifies that summons issued by a court shall be in writing, in duplicate, signed by the presiding officer, and bear the court's seal.
sections.key_rule: An unsigned or unsealed summons is defective. Both duplicates must be prepared — one for service, one retained by the court.

sections.heading: Summons how served
sections.function: Prescribes the method of serving a summons — personal service is primary, requiring delivery to the person summoned and a signed acknowledgement.
sections.key_rule: Personal service on the accused himself is the primary and preferred method. Substituted service is permitted only when personal service is not possible.

sections.heading: Service when person summoned cannot be found
sections.function: Permits substituted service by leaving the summons with an adult male member of the family at the person's last known residence.
sections.key_rule: Substituted service is only available after personal service has genuinely failed. The server must certify the attempt and identify the recipient.

sections.heading: Service of summons outside local limits
sections.function: Provides that where a person summoned is outside the court's local limits, summons may be sent to a Magistrate having jurisdiction where the person resides.
sections.key_rule: Cross-jurisdictional service runs through the local court — not directly by the issuing court's officer.

sections.heading: Form of warrant of arrest
sections.function: Specifies that a warrant of arrest must be in writing, signed by the presiding officer, and bear the court's seal.
sections.key_rule: An arrest warrant must be signed and sealed. An unsigned warrant is not a valid warrant.

sections.heading: Warrant to whom directed
sections.function: Provides that a warrant shall be directed to one or more police officers, or to any person specifically named where no police officer is available.
sections.key_rule: Ordinary warrants go to police. Non-police execution requires specific naming in the warrant.

sections.heading: Warrant directed to police officer for execution outside jurisdiction
sections.function: Permits a police officer to execute a warrant anywhere in Pakistan, but requires endorsement by a local Magistrate when executing outside the issuing court's district.
sections.key_rule: Endorsement by local Magistrate is required before execution in another district.

sections.heading: Notification of substance of warrant
sections.function: Requires the officer executing a warrant to notify the person to be arrested of the substance of the warrant, and to show the warrant on demand.
sections.key_rule: Two separate obligations: (1) notify substance — mandatory always; (2) show warrant — mandatory on demand. Both must be satisfied.

sections.heading: Where warrant may be executed
sections.function: States that a warrant may be executed at any place in Pakistan.
sections.key_rule: A warrant does not expire at a jurisdictional boundary. It is valid throughout Pakistan.

sections.heading: Power to take bond for appearance
sections.function: Allows the officer in charge of a police station to take a bond from an arrested person who is to be produced before a court on a specific date.
sections.key_rule: This is the statutory basis for police bail before production — a practical tool to manage custody between arrest and Magistrate production.

sections.heading: Issue of warrant in lieu of or in addition to summons
sections.function: Empowers a court to issue a warrant instead of or in addition to a summons where the accused has failed to appear or is likely to abscond.
sections.key_rule: A summons may be converted to a warrant when the accused ignores process.

sections.heading: Power to take bail
sections.function: Allows a court issuing a warrant to endorse it with a direction that the accused be released on bail if he can provide satisfactory surety.
sections.key_rule: Bailable endorsement on a warrant means the executing officer may release the person without bringing him to court if bail is given at the time of arrest.

sections.heading: Summons to produce document or other thing
sections.function: Empowers a court or police officer to issue a summons requiring a person to produce a document or other thing at a specified time and place.
sections.key_rule: Production summons is not a warrant — it does not authorise arrest. Non-compliance may be followed by a search warrant under S.96.

sections.heading: When search warrant may issue
sections.function: Sets out the conditions under which a court may issue a search warrant — when a production summons would be futile or the thing is not in any specific person's possession.
sections.key_rule: Search warrant is a judicial response to the failure or anticipated failure of a production summons. It is not a first resort.

sections.heading: Issue of search warrant
sections.function: The primary search warrant provision — a Magistrate may issue a search warrant where he has reason to believe the conditions of S.93 are met.
sections.key_rule: Reason to believe must be genuine and preferably recorded. A search warrant issued without any articulated basis is vulnerable to challenge.

sections.heading: Search for persons wrongfully confined
sections.function: Allows a First Class Magistrate to issue a search warrant for a person believed to be wrongfully confined.
sections.key_rule: This is a civil-liberty protection provision — it allows judicial intervention to locate a wrongfully detained person. Related to habeas corpus but operable at Magistrate level.

sections.heading: Direction of search warrants
sections.function: Specifies that search warrants shall be directed to a police officer and executed with witnesses as per S.103.
sections.key_rule: The named officer executes the warrant. The S.103 witness requirements still apply.

sections.heading: Search to be made in presence of witnesses
sections.function: Requires that a search be conducted in the presence of two or more respectable inhabitants of the locality, who must sign a list of all things seized.
sections.key_rule: Two witnesses required — not police officers. Seizure list must be prepared and signed. Failure to comply undermines the evidential value of anything seized.
sections.note: This is the single most exam-relevant procedural rule in the search and seizure module.

chapters.title: Security for Keeping the Peace and Good Behaviour

sections.heading: Security for keeping the peace on conviction
sections.function: Empowers a court convicting a person of certain offences to require the person to execute a bond with sureties to keep the peace for up to three years.
sections.key_rule: Security bond is an additional order on top of the sentence — it does not replace it. Bond is for future good behaviour, not punishment.

sections.heading: Security for keeping the peace in other cases
sections.function: Empowers an Executive Magistrate to require any person to show cause why they should not be ordered to execute a bond to keep the peace, where information has been received that the person is likely to commit a breach of the peace.
sections.key_rule: Show-cause procedure must precede the order — no bond can be imposed without first giving the person an opportunity to respond.

sections.heading: Security for good behaviour from persons disseminating seditious matters
sections.function: Empowers a Magistrate to require security for good behaviour from a person who publishes seditious, inflammatory, or obscene material.
sections.key_rule: This is a preventive order triggered by publication, not conviction. It has significant constitutional implications for freedom of expression.
sections.note: S.108 has been challenged in several PLD-reported cases as a tool to suppress political dissent.

sections.heading: Security for good behaviour from habitual offenders
sections.function: Empowers a Magistrate to require a habitual offender to execute a good-behaviour bond for up to three years.
sections.key_rule: Habitual offender status must be established on evidence — prior convictions recorded. This is preventive, not punitive.

sections.heading: Order to be made
sections.function: Requires the Magistrate, when acting under S.107–110, to make an order in writing stating the substance of the information received, the amount of security, and the period.
sections.key_rule: A written order stating the basis is mandatory. An oral direction or an order that does not specify the basis is defective.

sections.heading: Inquiry as to truth of information
sections.function: Requires the Magistrate to conduct an inquiry into the truth of the information on which the security proceedings are based.
sections.key_rule: The inquiry must be genuine — the Magistrate cannot simply accept the information at face value. Evidence must be led and the person has a right to cross-examine.

sections.heading: Order to give security
sections.function: After the inquiry under S.116, if the Magistrate is satisfied that the order is necessary, he makes an order requiring the person to give security.
sections.key_rule: The bond must specify the amount, the period, and the sureties required.

sections.heading: Imprisonment in default of security
sections.function: Provides that a person who does not give security as ordered may be imprisoned until the end of the period for which security was required or until security is given.
sections.key_rule: Imprisonment in default is possible but must not exceed the specified period. The person may be released at any time on giving security.

sections.heading: Power to release persons imprisoned for failing to give security
sections.function: Empowers the Sessions Judge to release a person imprisoned under S.122 on the ground that the order was unnecessary.
sections.key_rule: This is a supervisory release mechanism — the Sessions Judge can override the Magistrate's security order and release the person.

sections.heading: Dispersal of assembly by use of civil force
sections.function: Empowers an Executive Magistrate or officer in charge of a police station to command an unlawful assembly to disperse, and to use civil force to disperse it if it does not comply.
sections.key_rule: The assembly must first be commanded to disperse. Use of force without prior command is unlawful.

sections.heading: Use of military force
sections.function: Empowers an Executive Magistrate to call upon armed forces to assist in dispersing an unlawful assembly that cannot be otherwise dispersed.
sections.key_rule: Military use requires a Magistrate's call — soldiers cannot act against an unlawful assembly without a Magistrate's direction under this section.

sections.heading: Power of senior officer of armed forces
sections.function: Where a Magistrate is not present, the senior officer of armed forces may order dispersal if he reasonably believes the assembly to be unlawful and dangerous.
sections.key_rule: Military power to act without a Magistrate is exceptional and requires imminent danger. The officer must consult the nearest Magistrate as soon as practicable.

sections.heading: Conditional order for removal of nuisance
sections.function: Empowers a District Magistrate to issue a conditional order requiring a person to remove a public nuisance — an unlawful obstruction, dangerous structure, or trade injurious to health — within a specified time.
sections.key_rule: Order is conditional: it calls upon the person to comply or show cause. It does not compel removal immediately — the show-cause stage comes first.

sections.heading: Procedure where he appears to show cause
sections.function: Where the person appears to show cause, the Magistrate conducts an inquiry to determine whether the nuisance exists and whether the order is appropriate.
sections.key_rule: The inquiry is quasi-judicial. Evidence is received from both sides. The Magistrate must give the person a full hearing.

sections.heading: Consequences of disobedience of order
sections.function: Provides that failure to comply with an absolute S.133 order is an offence, and the Magistrate may take action to remove the nuisance at the person's expense.
sections.key_rule: Non-compliance is itself a criminal offence. The Magistrate may also take direct remedial action and recover costs from the person.

sections.heading: Injunction pending inquiry
sections.function: Empowers the Magistrate to pass an interim injunction restraining the nuisance pending the inquiry under S.137.
sections.key_rule: Interim injunction may be passed without hearing the person if the nuisance is urgent. It is a holding measure pending the full hearing.

chapters.title: Urgent Cases of Nuisance or Apprehended Danger

sections.heading: Power to issue order in urgent cases of nuisance or apprehended danger
sections.function: Empowers a District Magistrate or Sub-Divisional Magistrate to issue an ex parte order prohibiting any person or class of persons from doing a specific act, where immediate prevention is necessary to prevent obstruction, injury to persons, or disturbance of public tranquility.
sections.key_rule: Order may be made ex parte and without notice where urgency requires it. Cannot initially exceed 2 months; may be extended up to 6 months. Must state material facts. Person affected may show cause.
sections.note: S.144 is one of the most constitutionally contested provisions in Pakistan. It has been used to prohibit assemblies, close markets, restrict movement, and ban publications. Each invocation must be justified on the specific facts — it cannot be used as a permanent blanket restriction.

sections.heading: Power to prohibit carrying arms in procession or mass drill
sections.function: Empowers a District Magistrate to prohibit, in any area, the carrying of arms in processions, the organising of military exercises by non-state groups, and similar paramilitary activities.
sections.key_rule: Prohibition must be by written order specifying the area and period. This is the preventive counterpart to the PPC provisions on unlawful assembly with weapons.

sections.heading: Procedure where dispute concerning land is likely to cause breach of peace
sections.function: Empowers an Executive Magistrate to attach disputed property and hold an inquiry into possession of land, water, or boundaries where a dispute is likely to cause a breach of the peace.
sections.key_rule: The Magistrate's jurisdiction is over possession only — not title. He determines who was in possession at the date of his order, not who is the legal owner.
sections.note: S.145 orders are frequently obtained and frequently appealed. The key limitation — possession, not ownership — is the most commonly misunderstood aspect.

sections.heading: Power to attach subject of dispute and to appoint receiver
sections.function: Empowers the Magistrate to attach the disputed property and appoint a receiver pending determination of possession.
sections.key_rule: Attachment prevents either party from altering the status quo. The receiver manages the property during the inquiry.

sections.heading: Disputes concerning rights of use of land or water
sections.function: Extends the S.145 procedure to disputes over rights of use of land or water that are likely to cause a breach of the peace.
sections.key_rule: Rights of use, not just possession of land, are within the Magistrate's S.147 jurisdiction.

sections.heading: Police to prevent cognizable offences
sections.function: Imposes a duty on every police officer to prevent the commission of cognizable offences within their jurisdiction.
sections.key_rule: This is a duty, not merely a power. A police officer who has prior knowledge of a cognizable offence and fails to prevent it may be held accountable.

sections.heading: Arrest to prevent offences
sections.function: Empowers a police officer to arrest without warrant any person designing to commit a cognizable offence, where there is no other means of preventing the commission.
sections.key_rule: Preventive arrest requires actual knowledge of design, not mere suspicion. It is a last resort when prevention cannot otherwise be achieved.

sections.heading: Prevention of injury to public property
sections.function: Empowers a police officer to prevent any person from damaging public property and to arrest without warrant any person who is in the act of committing such damage.
sections.key_rule: In-act arrest power for damage to public property — does not require a prior warrant even for non-cognizable forms of the damage.

chapters.title: Information to Police and Powers of Investigation

sections.heading: First Information Report
sections.function: Requires the officer in charge of a police station to record in writing information about a cognizable offence and to send a copy to the Magistrate.
sections.key_rule: Recording is mandatory on receipt of information — the officer has no discretion to refuse where the offence is cognizable.

sections.heading: Information as to non-cognizable cases and investigation of such cases
sections.function: Requires the officer to record information about non-cognizable offences and refer the complainant to the Magistrate. Investigation requires a Magistrate's prior order under S.155(2).
sections.key_rule: No investigation of a non-cognizable offence without a S.155(2) Magistrate's order. Investigation without this order is without jurisdiction.

sections.heading: Police officer's power to investigate cognizable cases
sections.function: Vests the investigating officer with power to investigate a cognizable offence without requiring any Magistrate's order.
sections.key_rule: Investigation authority is automatic on cognizable offence registration — no Magistrate's direction needed to begin.

sections.heading: Procedure for investigation
sections.function: Sets out the steps for investigation — the officer proceeds to the scene, ascertains facts, discovers the offender, and reports to the Magistrate.
sections.key_rule: The Magistrate must be informed of the investigation at the outset. The investigation is police-led but Magistrate-aware from day one.

sections.heading: Police officer's power to require attendance of witnesses
sections.function: Empowers the investigating officer to require the attendance before himself of any person who may be acquainted with the facts of the case.
sections.key_rule: Compelled attendance for examination — but the person cannot be compelled to incriminate himself. Women and children may be examined at their residence.

sections.heading: Examination of witnesses by police
sections.function: Governs the examination of witnesses during investigation — the officer may orally examine any person acquainted with the facts. Statements are recorded but cannot be signed.
sections.key_rule: S.161 statements are not admissible as substantive evidence — only to contradict or corroborate at trial. Police confessions are excluded absolutely under Art. 38 QSO.

sections.heading: Statements to police not to be signed
sections.function: Prohibits statements made to police during investigation from being signed by the maker, and limits their use at trial to contradiction or corroboration of the maker's oral testimony.
sections.key_rule: No signature on S.161 statements. Use at trial is limited to the narrow purposes of contradiction/corroboration only.

sections.heading: No inducement to be offered
sections.function: Prohibits a police officer from offering any inducement, threat, or promise to a person examined during investigation.
sections.key_rule: Any confession or statement obtained through inducement, threat, or promise is tainted. This section reinforces the voluntariness requirement for all statements.

sections.heading: Recording of confessions and statements
sections.function: Empowers any Magistrate to record confessions and statements during investigation after giving the mandatory warning about voluntariness.
sections.key_rule: A S.164 confession, properly recorded with the voluntary warning, is admissible as substantive evidence under Art. 40 QSO. This is the key distinction from S.161 statements.

sections.heading: Search by police officer
sections.function: Empowers the investigating officer to search without a warrant when he has grounds to believe that something necessary for the investigation may be found in a place, and delay would prejudice the investigation.
sections.key_rule: Grounds must be recorded before the search. Sending a copy to the nearest Magistrate is required. The S.103 witness requirement applies equally.

sections.heading: Procedure when investigation cannot be completed in twenty-four hours
sections.function: Governs remand — where investigation cannot be completed within 24 hours and the accused is in custody, the accused must be produced before the nearest Magistrate, who may authorise further detention.
sections.key_rule: Police custody is capped at 15 days total. Judicial custody up to 30 days total. After the period expires without a charge sheet, the accused is entitled to default bail under S.167(2).

sections.heading: Release of accused when evidence deficient
sections.function: Requires the officer in charge of a police station to release the accused if investigation reveals insufficient evidence to justify forwarding to a Magistrate — on a bond to appear if required.
sections.key_rule: Police cannot hold an accused indefinitely on insufficient evidence. Release on bond is mandatory when evidence is deficient.

sections.heading: Diary of proceedings in investigation
sections.function: Requires every officer conducting an investigation to maintain a daily diary recording the time of each step of investigation, the statements received, and the circumstances ascertained.
sections.key_rule: The case diary is a key document — it is the primary basis on which the Magistrate decides bail (S.497) and cognizance (S.190). Not automatically admissible in evidence but the court may use it.

sections.heading: Report of police officer
sections.function: Requires the officer to submit a report (charge sheet / challan) to the Magistrate on completion of investigation.
sections.key_rule: S.173 report is the formal close of investigation and the formal opening of judicial proceedings. A supplementary report under S.173(8) may be filed after the initial report.
sections.note: The distinction between the S.173 report's conclusion (no offence) and the Magistrate's independent power to take cognizance is a recurring exam and litigation point.

sections.heading: Police to enquire and report on suicide, etc.
sections.function: Requires the officer in charge of a police station to hold an inquest and report on deaths in suspicious circumstances, including deaths in police custody.
sections.key_rule: Inquest must be held where death is suspicious. Report must describe the apparent cause of death and record visible injuries.

sections.heading: Magistrate's power to hold inquiry
sections.function: Empowers a Magistrate to hold an independent inquiry into the cause of death whenever a person dies while in the custody of the police.
sections.key_rule: The Magistrate's inquiry is independent of the police inquest and is not contingent on the police finding suspicious circumstances. For custody deaths, this is the key judicial check.

chapters.title: Jurisdiction of Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials

sections.heading: Ordinary place of inquiry and trial
sections.function: Establishes that an offence shall ordinarily be inquired into and tried by a court within whose local jurisdiction it was committed.
sections.key_rule: Place of commission = place of trial, as the default rule. All exceptions to this rule are specifically provided for in subsequent sections.

sections.heading: Place of inquiry and trial where offence is committed partly in one place and partly in another
sections.function: Provides that where a continuing offence spans multiple jurisdictions, any court in whose area any part of the offence was committed has jurisdiction.
sections.key_rule: For offences with multiple loci — fraud, conspiracy, continuing offences — any court where any part of the act occurred has jurisdiction.

sections.heading: Offence consequent on another offence
sections.function: Where an act that causes an offence is done in one place and the consequence occurs in another, both courts have jurisdiction.
sections.key_rule: For result-based offences where cause and effect are in different jurisdictions, the accused may be tried in either.

sections.heading: High Court to decide, in case of doubt, where trial shall take place
sections.function: Empowers the High Court to determine the appropriate venue when there is doubt about which court has jurisdiction.
sections.key_rule: When jurisdiction is genuinely uncertain, the High Court resolves it. A lower court cannot simply decline jurisdiction without reference upward.

sections.heading: Offence committed outside Pakistan
sections.function: Provides that a person who commits an offence outside Pakistan and comes within Pakistan may be tried by any court having jurisdiction over the place where he is found.
sections.key_rule: Jurisdiction over extraterritorial offences attaches at the place of apprehension within Pakistan.

sections.heading: Cognizance of offences by Magistrates
sections.function: Sets out the three routes by which a Magistrate may take cognizance: (a) on a complaint; (b) on a police report; (c) on his own knowledge or information.
sections.key_rule: Cognizance is a judicial act — the Magistrate must apply his mind to the material. Taking cognizance begins the judicial phase of criminal proceedings.

sections.heading: Transfer on application of accused
sections.function: Empowers the Chief Judicial Magistrate to transfer a case from one Magistrate to another, on an application by the accused or suo motu.
sections.key_rule: Transfer is used to address bias, inconvenience, or administrative efficiency. The accused must show cause for transfer.

sections.heading: Sessions Court's power of cognizance
sections.function: Specifies that a Sessions Court may take cognizance only on commitment from a Magistrate, or on a reference from the High Court — it has no original cognizance power.
sections.key_rule: Sessions Court cannot take original cognizance. It receives cases from Magistrates (commitment) or the High Court (reference).

chapters.title: Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings

sections.heading: Prosecution for contempt of lawful authority of public servants etc.
sections.function: Prohibits a court from taking cognizance of certain offences (contempt of court, false evidence, offences against public servants) unless the relevant public servant or court files a complaint.
sections.key_rule: These offences require a specific complainant — the aggrieved public servant or court. A private party cannot set the prosecution in motion independently.
sections.note: S.195 has been a source of significant litigation — the question of whether a particular offence requires prior sanction affects cognizance jurisdiction.

sections.heading: Prosecution for offences against the state, etc.
sections.function: Requires prior sanction of the Federal or Provincial Government for prosecution of offences against the state, offences involving sedition, and certain other political offences.
sections.key_rule: No court shall take cognizance of these offences without prior government sanction. Absence of sanction is a jurisdictional defect, not a curable irregularity.

sections.heading: Prosecution of Judges and public servants
sections.function: Requires prior sanction of the Federal or Provincial Government for prosecution of a Judge or public servant for any offence alleged to have been committed in the discharge of official duty.
sections.key_rule: Sanction is required only where the act complained of was done in discharge of official duty — acts clearly outside official duty do not require sanction.
sections.note: The test for whether an act was in 'discharge of official duty' has generated substantial case law. The trend is to construe this broadly to protect officials acting in good faith.

sections.heading: Prosecution for offences against marriage
sections.function: Restricts cognizance of offences against marriage to complaints by the aggrieved party.
sections.key_rule: Only the person directly wronged by the offence may complain — not the police, not a third party.

sections.heading: Prosecution for defamation
sections.function: Restricts cognizance of defamation to complaints by the person defamed, with exceptions for defamation of the President and other high officials.
sections.key_rule: Defamation is a private-complainant offence — the State cannot prosecute for ordinary defamation.

sections.heading: Examination of complainant
sections.function: Requires the Magistrate to examine the complainant and present witnesses on oath and reduce the substance to writing before taking cognizance on a private complaint.
sections.key_rule: Examination is mandatory and must precede cognizance on a private complaint. Not required where the complainant is a public servant acting in official capacity.

sections.heading: Procedure by Magistrate not competent to take cognizance
sections.function: Requires a Magistrate who lacks jurisdiction to return the complaint to the complainant with direction to present it before the proper court.
sections.key_rule: An incompetent Magistrate cannot simply dismiss a complaint — he must direct it to the correct forum.

sections.heading: Postponement of issue of process
sections.function: Empowers the Magistrate to postpone issuing process to the accused and to inquire into the case himself or direct an investigation.
sections.key_rule: S.202 inquiry is a filter stage — the Magistrate uses it to assess whether sufficient grounds exist before summoning the accused. It guards against vexatious complaints.

sections.heading: Dismissal of complaint
sections.function: Empowers the Magistrate to dismiss a complaint if, after inquiry, he finds no sufficient ground to proceed.
sections.key_rule: Dismissal under S.203 is not an acquittal. The complainant may re-file if new material becomes available.

sections.heading: Issue of process
sections.function: Requires the Magistrate to issue summons or warrant to the accused where he finds sufficient ground for proceeding.
sections.key_rule: This is the formal step that makes the accused a party to the proceedings. Sufficient ground — not proof of guilt — is the threshold.

sections.heading: Magistrate may dispense with personal attendance of accused
sections.function: Empowers the Magistrate to allow the accused to appear by pleader instead of in person, where the offence is a minor one.
sections.key_rule: Dispensation of personal attendance is a convenience measure for minor offences. For serious offences, personal presence is required for plea and identification.

sections.heading: Supply to accused of copy of police report, etc.
sections.function: Requires the Magistrate to supply to the accused, free of cost, copies of the police report, FIR, statements of witnesses, and other relevant documents before trial.
sections.key_rule: The right to receive copies is mandatory before the accused is asked to enter a plea. Denial of copies vitiates the subsequent proceedings.

sections.heading: Procedure for trial of warrant cases on police report
sections.function: Sets out the procedure for warrant cases initiated by police report — the Magistrate considers documents, hears submissions, and frames charge or discharges.
sections.key_rule: In a police-report warrant case, the Magistrate must frame charge or discharge before recording evidence.

sections.heading: Commitment to Sessions Court
sections.function: Sets out the commitment procedure — where the Magistrate considers the case should be tried by the Sessions Court, he commits the accused.
sections.key_rule: Commitment is not a trial — the Magistrate does not assess guilt. He assesses jurisdiction: should this case go to Sessions?

sections.heading: Contents of charge
sections.function: Requires a charge to state the specific offence with which the accused is charged, using its specific name where the law gives one.
sections.key_rule: A charge without the specific legal name of the offence is defective. Name + section number + essential facts = minimum content.

sections.heading: Particulars as to time, place and person
sections.function: Requires the charge to state the time, place, and person or thing involved in the offence to the extent necessary to give the accused sufficient notice.
sections.key_rule: The particulars requirement is calibrated to notice — enough detail to identify the incident, not a blow-by-blow account.

sections.heading: Effect of errors
sections.function: Provides that no error in stating the offence shall be regarded as material unless it has caused a failure of justice.
sections.key_rule: Error + failure of justice = material. Error alone = immaterial. This prevents charge defects from becoming automatic acquittals.

sections.heading: Court may alter charge
sections.function: Empowers the court to alter or add to a charge at any time before judgment.
sections.key_rule: The power to alter is broad — any time, any reason — but the obligation to give the accused opportunity to meet the new charge is triggered immediately.

sections.heading: Person once convicted or acquitted not to be tried for the same offence
sections.function: Gives statutory expression to the double jeopardy principle — no person shall be tried for the same offence more than once.
sections.key_rule: A person acquitted or convicted of an offence may not be retried for the same offence or on the same facts. This implements Art. 13(a) of the Constitution.
sections.note: S.232 is the CrPC statement of double jeopardy. Art. 13(a) is the constitutional statement. Both operate together.

sections.heading: Conviction of offence not charged
sections.function: Permits a court to convict a person of an offence that was not the one charged, but whose facts were disclosed by the evidence.
sections.key_rule: A court may convict of an uncharged offence only where the accused had notice of the facts and the uncharged offence is supported by those facts.

sections.heading: When person charged with one offence can be convicted of another
sections.function: Provides specific rules for the circumstances in which a person charged with one offence may be convicted of another, lesser, or alternative offence.
sections.key_rule: Specific substitution rules: charged with murder → can convict of culpable homicide; charged with robbery → can convict of theft; and so on by schedule.

sections.heading: Framing of charge
sections.function: Requires the court to frame a charge at the commencement of a trial, based on the materials before it, unless the accused is discharged.
sections.key_rule: Charge framing is a formal judicial act — it defines the scope of the trial. Reading and explaining the charge to the accused must follow immediately.

chapters.title: Trial of Warrant Cases by Magistrates

sections.heading: When accused shall be discharged
sections.function: Empowers the Magistrate to discharge the accused at any stage of the proceedings where the charge is groundless.
sections.key_rule: Discharge is available at any time — it is not limited to the pre-evidence stage. Groundlessness must be assessed on materials actually before the court.

sections.heading: Procedure for taking cognizance and framing of charge in warrant cases
sections.function: Sets out the specific procedure for warrant cases — after hearing the parties, the Magistrate either frames a charge and records the accused's plea, or discharges.
sections.key_rule: Two outcomes at this stage: charge + plea, or discharge. There is no intermediate option.

sections.heading: Evidence for prosecution
sections.function: Governs the recording of prosecution evidence in a warrant case — the Magistrate takes evidence and the accused may cross-examine.
sections.key_rule: Each prosecution witness is examined, cross-examined, and re-examined before the next witness is called. The accused has an absolute right of cross-examination at this stage.

sections.heading: Evidence for defence
sections.function: Governs the defence evidence stage in a warrant case — the accused may give evidence himself, call witnesses, and present written statements.
sections.key_rule: The accused cannot be compelled to give evidence. If he does, he becomes a witness and may be cross-examined. Silence cannot be used against him.

sections.heading: Procedure in summons cases
sections.function: Sets out the simplified procedure for summons cases — the accused is asked whether guilty and if he pleads guilty, the Magistrate may convict.
sections.key_rule: The Magistrate must be satisfied the plea is voluntary before recording a guilty conviction. An involuntary or conditional plea must be treated as a not-guilty plea.

sections.heading: Non-appearance or death of complainant
sections.function: Governs what happens if the complainant does not appear at the hearing or dies — the Magistrate may acquit the accused in a complaint case.
sections.key_rule: Non-appearance of complainant enables acquittal in a complaint case. For police cases, prosecution continues irrespective of complainant attendance.

sections.heading: Compensation for accusation without reasonable cause
sections.function: Empowers the Magistrate to order a complainant who made a false or vexatious complaint to pay compensation to the acquitted accused.
sections.key_rule: Compensation requires a finding of no reasonable cause — it is not automatic on acquittal. The court must find the complaint was brought without adequate grounds.

sections.heading: Opening case for prosecution
sections.function: Requires the prosecution to open its case by describing the charge and evidence it proposes to adduce before calling witnesses.
sections.key_rule: Opening statement is mandatory in Sessions trials — it gives the court a roadmap and locks the prosecution into its stated case.

sections.heading: Discharge
sections.function: Empowers the Sessions Court to discharge the accused where, after hearing the prosecution opening, it considers the charge to be groundless.
sections.key_rule: Discharge at Sessions level follows opening — it is the Sessions Court's equivalent of the Magistrate's pre-evidence discharge power.

sections.heading: Framing of charge
sections.function: Requires the Sessions Court to frame a charge where the materials disclose a prima facie case.
sections.key_rule: The Sessions Court independently frames the charge — it is not bound by the charge in the commitment order.

sections.heading: Evidence for prosecution
sections.function: Governs recording of prosecution evidence at Sessions trial — witnesses are examined, cross-examined, and re-examined.
sections.key_rule: The accused has an absolute right to cross-examine every prosecution witness. Denial of cross-examination is a fundamental procedural defect.

sections.heading: Acquittal
sections.function: Empowers the Sessions Court to acquit the accused after prosecution evidence if the evidence does not disclose the offence charged.
sections.key_rule: Acquittal after prosecution evidence — before defence evidence is called — is available where the prosecution has failed to make out a prima facie case.

sections.heading: Entering upon defence
sections.function: After prosecution evidence is complete, the accused is asked whether he wishes to call witnesses or give evidence himself.
sections.key_rule: The accused cannot be compelled to testify or call witnesses. If he testifies, he may be cross-examined. Silence is not evidence of guilt.

sections.heading: Judgment
sections.function: Requires the Sessions Court to pronounce judgment after arguments, either acquitting or convicting the accused.
sections.key_rule: Judgment must be pronounced in open court. The provisions of S.366–371 governing form, reasons, and signature apply.

sections.heading: Power to try summarily
sections.function: Specifies which Magistrates are empowered to try cases summarily and which categories of offences may be so tried.
sections.key_rule: Only empowered Magistrates may use the summary procedure. Only specified offences qualify. A Magistrate without empowerment or trying an unqualified offence is without jurisdiction.

sections.heading: Procedure for summary trials
sections.function: Provides that the procedure for summary trials follows the ordinary summons-case procedure, with modifications in the recording of evidence.
sections.key_rule: Summary trial follows summons-case rules — no formal charge framing required. The main difference is the abbreviated record under S.263.

sections.heading: Record in summary trials
sections.function: Requires the Magistrate in a summary trial to record the substance of evidence, the finding, and the sentence in a prescribed form — not verbatim testimony.
sections.key_rule: Substance of evidence, not verbatim record. This is what makes summary trials fast. An inadequate record is a ground of appeal.

sections.heading: No appeal in certain cases
sections.function: Provides that there is no appeal from a summary conviction where the sentence is a fine only, not exceeding a prescribed maximum.
sections.key_rule: Small-fine-only summary convictions are non-appealable. Revision remains available for jurisdictional or legal errors.

sections.heading: Definitions — Plea Bargaining
sections.function: Defines key terms for the plea bargaining chapter and specifies the categories of cases in which plea bargaining is available.
sections.key_rule: Available only to first-time offenders and only for offences not involving death sentence, life imprisonment, or offences against the state. Repeat offenders and terrorists are excluded.
sections.note: Plea bargaining was introduced by the Criminal Law (Amendment) Act 2007. It is a relatively new addition to Pakistani criminal procedure and is increasingly tested in modern LLB exams.

sections.heading: Application for plea bargaining
sections.function: Sets out the procedure for initiating plea bargaining — the accused files an application before the trial court at any time after the charge is framed.
sections.key_rule: Plea bargaining can only begin after charge framing. An application before charge is premature.

sections.heading: Hearing on the application
sections.function: Requires the court to hold a hearing with both parties and examine the accused to ensure he understands plea bargaining and has applied voluntarily.
sections.key_rule: Voluntariness is the key safeguard. The court cannot approve plea bargaining unless satisfied the accused is acting freely, with full understanding, and without compulsion.

sections.heading: Mutually satisfactory disposition
sections.function: Requires the court to facilitate a mutually acceptable resolution between prosecution and accused, which may include the accused agreeing to pay compensation or return property.
sections.key_rule: Plea bargaining in Pakistan often involves restitution or compensation as part of the bargain — not just a reduced sentence.

sections.heading: Judgment of the court in case of plea bargaining
sections.function: Requires the court to pronounce judgment in accordance with the plea bargaining agreement.
sections.key_rule: The court must implement the agreement — it cannot approve the bargain and then impose a different sentence.

sections.heading: Finality of judgment
sections.function: Provides that a plea bargaining judgment is final and binding — no appeal lies.
sections.key_rule: No appeal from a plea bargaining conviction. This is the trade-off for the reduced sentence. Revision for jurisdictional errors remains theoretically available.

sections.heading: Trial of cases where application for plea bargaining is rejected
sections.function: Provides that where plea bargaining is rejected or fails, the trial proceeds from where it was before the application.
sections.key_rule: Failed plea bargaining does not prejudice the trial — the statements made during plea bargaining negotiations cannot be used as evidence at the resumed trial.

sections.heading: Judgment of conviction or acquittal to be pronounced in open court
sections.function: Requires judgment to be pronounced in open court, in the presence of the accused for conviction cases.
sections.key_rule: Open court pronouncement is mandatory. Judgment delivered in chambers or in the accused's absence (for convictions) is procedurally defective.

sections.heading: Language and contents of judgment
sections.function: Requires judgment to be written, dated, signed, and to contain the points for determination, the decision, and the reasons.
sections.key_rule: Reasoned judgment is mandatory. A bare verdict without reasons is deficient under S.367 and can be set aside on appeal.

sections.heading: Order to pay compensation
sections.function: Empowers the court to order the accused, on conviction, to pay compensation to the victim for injuries suffered, in addition to any fine.
sections.key_rule: Compensation is distinct from fine — it goes to the victim, not the state. The court must consider the accused's means when setting the amount.

sections.heading: Court not to alter judgment
sections.function: Provides that once a judgment is signed, no court shall alter or review it except to correct a clerical or arithmetic error.
sections.key_rule: Signed judgment is final at that court's level. Substantive correction requires an appeal or revision, not amendment of the original judgment.

sections.heading: Copy of judgment to be given to accused
sections.function: Entitles a convicted accused to a copy of the judgment free of charge.
sections.key_rule: The right is unconditional and free of cost. Denial of the judgment copy undermines the right of appeal.

chapters.title: Submission of Death Sentences for Confirmation

sections.heading: Death sentence to be confirmed by High Court
sections.function: Requires every death sentence passed by a Sessions Court to be submitted to the High Court for confirmation before execution.
sections.key_rule: Confirmation is mandatory and initiated by the Sessions Court itself. The accused need not file anything — the duty to submit is on the court.

sections.heading: Power of High Court in confirmatory proceedings
sections.function: Sets out the High Court's powers in confirmation — it may confirm, reduce, alter, annul, or order retrial.
sections.key_rule: The High Court's confirmatory power is comprehensive — it may acquit, reduce the sentence, or substitute a non-capital conviction.

sections.heading: Confirmation or new sentence to be signed by two judges
sections.function: Requires a High Court confirmation or new sentence in capital cases to be signed by at least two judges.
sections.key_rule: Capital confirmation requires a minimum of two judges — a single judge cannot confirm a death sentence.

sections.heading: Warrant for execution of sentence of death
sections.function: Provides that the Sessions Court, after receiving the High Court's confirmation, issues the death warrant specifying the date and place of execution.
sections.key_rule: The death warrant is issued by the sentencing court, not the confirming court. It follows confirmation and specifies the execution date.

sections.heading: Execution of sentence of death
sections.function: Provides that the sentence of death shall be executed by hanging in the manner prescribed.
sections.key_rule: The mode of execution is prescribed by law — it cannot be varied by the court. Only the Central Government can commute the mode.

sections.heading: Execution of sentence of death on pregnant woman
sections.function: Prohibits execution of a death sentence on a pregnant woman — the sentence shall be commuted to life imprisonment.
sections.key_rule: Pregnancy is an absolute bar to execution. The commutation to life imprisonment is mandatory on the pregnancy being confirmed.

sections.heading: Execution of sentence of imprisonment
sections.function: Provides for the execution of sentences of imprisonment — the accused is committed to prison under a warrant.
sections.key_rule: A warrant of commitment must accompany every person committed to prison on a criminal sentence. Imprisonment without warrant is unlawful.

sections.heading: Levy of fine
sections.function: Provides the procedure for collecting fines — by attachment and sale of moveable property, arrest and imprisonment in default, or from property of deceased convict.
sections.key_rule: Fines are collected progressively — moveable property first, then immoveable. Imprisonment in default is a last resort.

sections.heading: Offences punishable with imprisonment for life
sections.function: Provides that where a law prescribes imprisonment for life, the court shall sentence to such imprisonment and shall not commute it.
sections.key_rule: Imprisonment for life is a fixed sentence — courts cannot reduce it to a term of years on sentencing. Only the executive can commute.

chapters.title: Suspensions, Remissions and Commutations of Sentences

sections.heading: Power to suspend or remit sentences
sections.function: Empowers the Provincial Government to suspend, remit, or commute the sentence of any person convicted of any offence, with or without conditions.
sections.key_rule: The executive power to remit is unlimited except for offences where the law specifically restricts it. It is not subject to court oversight after the fact.
sections.note: S.401 remission powers interact with Art. 45 (Presidential pardon) and the separation of powers — significant constitutional dimensions.

sections.heading: Power of Federal Government to suspend or remit in certain cases
sections.function: Reserves certain remission and commutation powers to the Federal Government — particularly for offences under Federal laws and death sentences.
sections.key_rule: Federal vs Provincial remission powers track the Federal vs Provincial legislative powers. Capital sentence commutation belongs primarily to the Federal Government through Art. 45.

sections.heading: Person once convicted or acquitted not to be tried for the same offence
sections.function: Restates the double jeopardy protection — a person who has been tried for an offence by a court of competent jurisdiction cannot be tried again for the same offence.
sections.key_rule: This is the CrPC double jeopardy provision complementing Art. 13(a) of the Constitution. A previous trial by a competent court bars retrial.

sections.heading: In what cases bail to be taken
sections.function: Establishes the right to bail in bailable offences — the officer in charge of the police station or the court must release on bail as a matter of right.
sections.key_rule: Bail in bailable offences is a right, not a discretion. The officer or court has no power to refuse it.

sections.heading: When bail may be taken in case of non-bailable offence
sections.function: Governs bail in non-bailable offences — a Magistrate or Sessions Court may grant bail subject to the statutory bar where reasonable grounds exist for a serious offence.
sections.key_rule: Bail is the rule; the statutory bar is the exception. Azizullah Memon (PLD 1993 SC 341) = bail is presumptive even in non-bailable cases.

sections.heading: Power of High Court or Court of Session to grant bail
sections.function: Gives the High Court and Sessions Court an independent and unrestricted power to grant bail in any case.
sections.key_rule: The High Court's bail power under S.498 is wider than the Magistrate's S.497 power — it is not subject to the same statutory bar and is exercised with broader discretion.

sections.heading: Amount of bond
sections.function: Governs the amount of the bail bond and the number of sureties — must be proportionate to the offence and the accused's means.
sections.key_rule: Excessive bail is constitutionally impermissible. The court must take the accused's means into account when setting the bond amount.

sections.heading: Discharge from custody
sections.function: Requires the officer in charge of the jail or police station to release the accused as soon as the bail bond is executed.
sections.key_rule: Once the bond is properly executed, release is mandatory and immediate — there is no residual discretion to detain.

sections.heading: Discharge of sureties
sections.function: Empowers a surety to discharge himself by causing the accused to be arrested and produced before the court.
sections.key_rule: A surety who can no longer guarantee the accused's appearance can effectively hand the accused back to the court. The accused then reverts to custody unless fresh bail is given.

sections.heading: No appeal in certain cases
sections.function: Specifies categories of cases in which no appeal lies — primarily petty convictions where the sentence is a fine below a specified amount.
sections.key_rule: The right of appeal is statutory, not inherent. Where S.404 excludes it, there is no appeal — only revision.

sections.heading: Appeals to High Court
sections.function: Provides for appeals from Sessions Court convictions and sentences to the High Court.
sections.key_rule: S.410 is the foundational provision for the accused's right to appeal to the High Court from a Sessions conviction.

sections.heading: Powers of appellate court
sections.function: Sets out what an appellate court may do: confirm, reverse, alter the finding or sentence, order retrial, or take additional evidence.
sections.key_rule: The appellate court's power is comprehensive — it can do anything the original court could have done, and more.

sections.heading: Power of High Court to enhance sentence
sections.function: Empowers the High Court to enhance a sentence on an appeal against conviction, or suo motu where a Sessions Court has imposed an inadequate sentence.
sections.key_rule: Sentence enhancement on an accused's own appeal (without prosecution cross-appeal) is technically available but courts exercise it cautiously to avoid discouraging appeals.

sections.heading: Execution of sentence pending appeal by convict
sections.function: Provides that an appeal does not automatically stay execution of sentence — a separate stay application must be made.
sections.key_rule: Filing an appeal does not suspend the sentence. A specific stay of execution must be obtained from the appellate court.

sections.heading: Stay of sentence
sections.function: Empowers the appellate court to suspend execution of the sentence and release the accused on bail pending the appeal.
sections.key_rule: Stay + bail pending appeal are separate from the substantive appeal. They may be granted or refused independently of the merits.

sections.heading: Government's right of appeal against acquittal
sections.function: Empowers the Provincial Government (through the Public Prosecutor) to appeal against an order of acquittal to the High Court.
sections.key_rule: The government, not a private complainant, holds the appeal right against acquittal. A private complainant may only seek leave to appeal through the government.

sections.heading: Appeals from convictions
sections.function: Sets out the full hierarchy of appeals from convictions — to Sessions Court (from Magistrate), to High Court (from Sessions), with the Supreme Court as final option on leave.
sections.key_rule: The appellate ladder is: Magistrate → Sessions → High Court → Supreme Court (leave required). Each step must ordinarily be exhausted before the next is available.

sections.heading: Summary dismissal of appeal
sections.function: Empowers the appellate court to summarily dismiss an appeal without full hearing if it appears to have no merit.
sections.key_rule: Summary dismissal is possible without hearing the appellant in full. The court must, however, examine the petition and record before dismissing.

sections.heading: Powers of appellate court
sections.function: Sets out the full range of powers of appellate courts when disposing of appeals — confirmation, reversal, modification, acquittal, retrial.
sections.key_rule: An appellate court may do anything the original court could have done, and may also order a fresh trial before a different court.

sections.heading: Revision generally
sections.function: Sets out the framework for revisional jurisdiction — the High Court and Sessions Court may call for the record of any lower court and examine it for correctness, legality, or propriety.
sections.key_rule: Revision is not an appeal — it is a supervisory power. It covers correctness of law, not purely factual disputes.

sections.heading: Sessions Judge's powers of revision
sections.function: Specifies the Sessions Judge's powers in revision — he may examine the record, pass such order as justice requires, but cannot convert an acquittal into a conviction.
sections.key_rule: Sessions Judge in revision cannot convert acquittal to conviction — that requires the High Court's power. He may, however, enhance sentences.

sections.heading: High Court's power of revision
sections.function: Sets out the High Court's revisional powers — the widest in the system, including power to call for any record, examine, alter, annul, or substitute any finding.
sections.key_rule: The High Court's revision power is comprehensive. It includes power to act on its own motion without any application.

sections.heading: Sessions Judge's power to revise Magistrate's orders
sections.function: Sets out the Sessions Judge's specific power to call for and examine the records of any Magistrate in his sessions division.
sections.key_rule: The Sessions Judge's revisional power covers all Magistrates in his division. He may modify orders, pass appropriate orders, or refer to the High Court.

sections.heading: High Court's power to transfer cases and appeals
sections.function: Empowers the High Court to transfer any case or appeal from one court to another for reasons of convenience, fairness, or judicial efficiency.
sections.key_rule: Transfer requires reasons. Mere preference of the parties is insufficient. Bias, inconvenience to witnesses, or connected litigation in another court are standard grounds.

sections.heading: Sessions Judge's power to transfer cases
sections.function: Empowers the Sessions Judge to transfer cases between Magistrates within his division.
sections.key_rule: Administrative transfer by Sessions Judge does not require specific cause — it is an administrative management power over the Magistracy.

sections.heading: Transfer on application of accused
sections.function: Provides for transfer of a case on application by the accused where he can show a fair trial is impossible in the current court.
sections.key_rule: The applicant must show a real possibility of bias or injustice — a subjective apprehension is insufficient. Objective grounds are required.

sections.heading: Power to issue directions of the nature of habeas corpus
sections.function: Empowers the High Court to issue directions requiring the body of a person detained to be produced before it, to inquire into the legality of the detention.
sections.key_rule: S.491 is the CrPC habeas corpus provision, separate from the constitutional Art. 199 writ. It is available for any unlawful detention, not just detention by the state.
sections.note: The relationship between S.491 CrPC habeas corpus and Art. 199 constitutional habeas corpus is an important distinction. Art. 199 is broader and cannot be ousted by statute.

sections.heading: Irregularities which vitiate proceedings
sections.function: Lists specific procedural errors that automatically vitiate criminal proceedings — jurisdiction errors, absence of required complaint, non-compliance with mandatory sanction.
sections.key_rule: Errors in S.537's list are automatically fatal — no need to show prejudice. These are the hard limits where the cure provisions do not apply.

sections.heading: Irregularities which do not vitiate proceedings
sections.function: Lists procedural errors that do not automatically void proceedings — error in charge, mis-description of property, defect in summons — provided no failure of justice results.
sections.key_rule: These errors are curable unless they actually caused prejudice. Read alongside S.225 (effect of errors in charges).

sections.heading: Power to summon material witness or examine person present
sections.function: Empowers the court to summon any material witness or examine any person present in court, at any stage of a trial, even without any party's application.
sections.key_rule: The court's duty to discover truth is independent of the parties — it may call witnesses neither party has called. A broad inquisitorial power within the adversarial system.

sections.heading: Inherent powers of High Court
sections.function: Preserves the High Court's inherent power to make any order necessary to give effect to any order under the Code, prevent abuse of the process of any court, or otherwise secure the ends of justice.
sections.key_rule: S.561-A fills gaps and prevents abuse. It cannot be used to override express statutory provisions or to reopen concluded proceedings through the back door.
sections.note: S.561-A is one of the most litigated provisions in the entire CrPC. Key limits: it cannot be used where a specific remedy exists, and it cannot override a specific statutory prohibition.
